paragraphs: Archipal (also spelled Archipel, and known as De L'Archipel) is an heirloom common fig of French and English heritage that reached California in 1889. Its synonyms include Ronde Noire, Figue Grise and Hardy Prolific, and it is sometimes confused with Osborn. It bears large, pear-shaped fruit with bronze skin tinged with violet.

The flesh is amber coloured and honey-flavoured, and the fruit is at its best eaten fresh. A mid-season variety, Archipal performs best in cool coastal conditions rather than hot inland areas, where the fruit is prone to spoiling.

claims.text: An heirloom common fig of French and English heritage that reached California in 1889; synonyms include De L'Archipel, Ronde Noire, Figue Grise and Hardy Prolific, and it is sometimes confused with Osborn
Bears large, pear-shaped fruit with bronze skin tinged with violet
Flesh is amber coloured and honey-flavoured, and the fruit is best eaten fresh
A mid-season fig that performs best in cool coastal climates and is prone to spoiling in hot inland areas

paragraphs: Bedu is a common name for Ficus palmata, the Punjab fig or wild Himalayan fig. It is not the common fig (Ficus carica) but a wild relative of it, native across north-eastern Africa and Arabia through to northern India and the Himalayas, where its fruit is widely eaten.

It grows as a vigorous shrub or small tree, larger and more vigorous than the common fig, and bears small fruit (around 2 cm) that turns purplish as it ripens. The fruit is eaten fresh or dried. Ficus palmata is graft-compatible with Ficus carica and readily hybridises with it, which is part of its appeal to collectors.

claims.text: Bedu is a common name for Ficus palmata, the Punjab or wild Himalayan fig, a wild relative of the common fig Ficus carica
Native from north-eastern Africa and Arabia through to northern India and the Himalayas, where its fruit is widely eaten
Vigorous shrub or small tree bearing small fruit (around 2 cm) that turns purplish, eaten fresh or dried
Graft-compatible with Ficus carica and hybridises readily with it

paragraphs: Black Genoa is a large, pear-shaped fig with dark purple, almost black skin and deep red flesh. It has a very sweet, rich flavour and good keeping quality, which is why it is one of the leading commercial fresh-fig varieties grown in Australia.
Like most figs it can bear two crops a year, a small early breba crop on old wood and a heavier main crop on new growth, ripening through summer and autumn in Australia.

claims.text: Large pear-shaped fruit with dark purple to near-black skin and red flesh
Very sweet, rich flavour with good storage quality
A leading commercial fresh-fig variety in Australia, grown for high yields
Bears two crops a year (a spring breba crop and a main crop)

sources.name: Ultimate Backyard: Fig Tree Varieties in Australia

paragraphs: Black Mission, also called Mission or Franciscan, is one of the most historic figs in cultivation. It came from Spain and was planted by Franciscan missionaries at San Diego in 1768, then spread up the California coast as new missions were built. The fruit has a dark purple to almost black skin and a strawberry to amber red interior. A telltale trait is that the skin often cracks or checks at full maturity.

It is a reliable two crop fig: a large, pyriform breba crop early, then a medium sized main crop later in the season. The flavour is rich and distinctly figgy, and the quality is rated excellent both fresh and dried, which made it a commercial mainstay in California. Its sweet, jammy flesh dries well, and it remains a benchmark dark fig for fresh eating.

claims.text: Black Mission (Mission, Franciscan) came from Spain and was planted by Franciscan missionaries at San Diego in 1768, then spread up the California coast.
The skin is dark purple to almost black and the interior is strawberry to amber red.
The skin often cracks or checks at full maturity.
It produces two crops, a large breba crop early and a medium sized main crop later.
Quality is excellent both fresh and dried.

paragraphs: Blue Provence is an old heritage fig with a long history in Australia. The earliest documented reference is from 1869, when plants were sourced from the Camden Park nursery in New South Wales, and it later appeared in early Australian fruit catalogues such as Goodman's. It is recorded under several synonyms, including Adam, Perth Adam, Augusta Purple and Adams Pride, so the same fig circulates under more than one name.

The fruit has blue to violet skin with prominent ribs and an open eye, over soft, very sweet flesh with a purple-blue tinge and red pulp. The flavour sits in the dark-berry camp, sweet with distinct berry notes and a touch of acidity. It grows as a large, vigorous, large-leaved tree and ripens mid to late season, roughly March to April in Australian conditions.

claims.text: Earliest documented Australian reference dates to 1869 from the Camden Park nursery in New South Wales, with later listings in early Australian fruit catalogues
Recorded under synonyms including Adam, Perth Adam, Augusta Purple and Adams Pride
Blue to violet skin with prominent ribs and an open eye, with soft red pulp showing a purple-blue tinge
Dark-berry type with very sweet, soft flesh, distinct berry notes and a touch of acidity; noted as sweeter than many figs
Ripens mid to late season, around March to April in Australia

paragraphs: Bourjassotte Noire is an old Mediterranean fig whose name traces to Burjassot near Valencia in Spain, though it has been grown around the western Mediterranean for centuries. In France it is the variety behind the Sollies figs of Provence, which carry a Protected Designation of Origin (PDO). The skin is a dark purple to near black with violet tones at the neck and a bluish bloom, and it is ribbed, often checking and cracking as it ripens.

Inside, the flesh is a deep red, dense and tender. The flavour is rich and sweet with a balancing tartness, giving it a floral, berry like character that fig growers prize. The skin cracking at full ripeness is a classic sign of a sweet, well ripened fig. It is grown mainly as a premium fresh eating fig, the same fruit marketed in France as the Sollies violette.

claims.text: Bourjassotte Noire takes its name from Burjassot near Valencia, Spain, and has been grown around the western Mediterranean for centuries.
In France it is the variety behind the Sollies PDO figs of Provence.
The skin is dark purple to near black with violet neck tones and a bluish bloom, ribbed and checking or cracking when ripe.
The flesh is deep red, dense and tender.
The flavour is rich and sweet with balancing tartness and floral, berry like notes.

sources.name: Specialty Produce: Bourjasotte Noire Sollies Figs

paragraphs: Brown Turkey is a popular, adaptable fig whose fruit ripens to a coppery purple-brown skin with pink to amber pulp and a mild, sweet flavour. It is a reliable, prolific cropper often grown both commercially and in home gardens, and is sometimes sold as Texas Everbearing.
It can produce two crops in a season, an early breba crop and a later main crop, and the fruit is good eaten fresh or used for cooking and preserving.

claims.text: Ripens to coppery purple-brown skin with pink to amber pulp
Mild, sweet flavour, good eaten fresh or dried
Also known as Texas Everbearing
Can produce two harvests a year, an early breba crop and a main crop

sources.name: NC State Extension: Ficus carica Brown Turkey

paragraphs: Cape White is a very old white fig long associated with South Africa, where it is one of the oldest cultivars and the name by which it is best known. It is a small to medium, early ripening fig with green skin that turns only slightly yellow when fully ripe, and pale flesh (described as cream to light straw). In Australia it is grown across the eastern state fruit collections.

It is an early variety, ripening from about January in the southern hemisphere summer, well ahead of many other figs. The fruit has a sweet, delicate flavour and is especially popular for jam, while also being used fresh and for drying.

claims.text: Cape White is a very old fig cultivar long associated with South Africa, where the name originates
It is a small to medium, early ripening fig with green skin and pale (cream to light straw) flesh
It ripens from about January in the southern hemisphere summer
It has a sweet, delicate flavour and is especially popular for jam

sources.name: The Food Forest: Figs fact sheet

paragraphs: Celeste, widely known as the Sugar fig, is a small to medium fig long grown across the south eastern United States. It is often listed under the synonyms Celestial, Malta and Sugar fig. The skin ripens to a purplish bronze over light brown, and the flesh is rose to pink. Its standout trait is a tightly closed eye that resists insects and souring, which suits humid climates and keeps the fruit from splitting.

Flavour is the other draw. Celeste is intensely sweet and honeyed, smooth and rich, which is exactly why it earned the Sugar fig name. It is a common fig and self pollinating, so it sets fruit without a wasp. The crop ripens early, from late summer, making it one of the first figs of the season. Good eaten fresh or made into preserves.

claims.text: Celeste is a small to medium fig also called the Sugar fig, with synonyms including Celestial, Malta and Sugar fig.
The skin ripens to a purplish bronze over light brown and the flesh is rose to pink.
Celeste has a tightly closed eye that resists insects and souring and helps it not split.
The fruit is intensely sweet and honeyed with a smooth, rich flavour, the reason it is called the Sugar fig.
Celeste is a common fig, self pollinating, and ripens early in the season.
It is good eaten fresh or made into preserves.

paragraphs: Clown Mosaic refers to the Clown Fig or Mosaic Fig, the variegated form of Ficus aspera (often sold as 'Parcellii'). It is an ornamental relative of the edible fig, native to Vanuatu in the South Pacific and first described in scientific literature in 1786 from a specimen collected on Tanna Island. It is grown for its striking foliage rather than for fruit.

Its large, rough-textured leaves carry a highly variable mosaic of grey-green, cream and dull pink over dark green, and even the small figs are striped. The fruit is cauliflorous, forming directly on the trunk and branches and ripening from green through orange-red to purple. Tender and warmth-loving, it is usually grown as an ornamental foliage plant or container houseplant.

claims.text: An ornamental form of Ficus aspera (Clown Fig / Mosaic Fig), native to Vanuatu and first described in scientific literature in 1786 from a specimen collected on Tanna Island
The variegated 'Parcellii' form shows a highly variable mosaic of paler grey-green, cream and dull pink over dark green leaves
Fruit is cauliflorous, borne on the trunk and branches, the small figs are striped, and they ripen from green through orange-red to purple
Grown as an ornamental foliage plant rather than for fruit, and is tender, suited to tropical and subtropical conditions

sources.name: Top Tropicals: Ficus aspera (Variegated Clown Fig)

paragraphs: The Deciduous Fig is an Australian native strangler fig, Ficus henneana, found only in Australia from Milton in New South Wales north to Queensland and the Northern Territory, in riverine, coastal and drier rainforest. It was named after Diedrich Henne, who collected it at Booby Island in the Torres Strait in 1861, and in 2013 it was returned to the name Ficus henneana after earlier being treated as a variety of Ficus superba. It begins life as a hemi-epiphyte, can strangle its host, and reaches up to 35 metres on the best sites.

Unusually for a fig, it is semi-deciduous, rarely dropping every leaf but often seen almost bare in October, which gives it its common name. The small globular figs are about 25 millimetres across, purplish with pink dots, and ripen from January to July. They are edible but not especially palatable to people, so the tree is valued more as a food source for wildlife, feeding fig-eating birds such as the Australasian figbird, the rare Coxen's fig-parrot and the wompoo fruit dove.

claims.text: Ficus henneana (Deciduous Fig) occurs only in Australia, from Milton NSW to northern Queensland and the Northern Territory, in riverine, littoral and drier rainforest
Named after Diedrich Henne, who collected it at Booby Island in the Torres Strait in 1861; reassigned to the name Ficus henneana in 2013 after being treated as a variety of Ficus superba
A hemi-epiphyte strangler fig that can reach up to 35 metres, and is semi-deciduous, seldom losing all leaves but often nearly bare in October
Figs are globular, about 25 millimetres in diameter, purplish with pink dots, ripening January to July, edible for humans but not particularly palatable
Native rainforest tree whose figs are eaten by birds including the Australasian figbird, the rare Coxen's fig-parrot and the wompoo fruit dove

sources.name: Daleys Fruit Tree Nursery: Deciduous Fig (Ficus superba)

paragraphs: Flanders is a fig selected from the University of California fig breeding program run by Ira J. Condit at Riverside. The fruit has a long slender neck and a light tan to brownish-yellow skin marked with violet stripes, over amber to pink pulp.

It is prized for rich, sweet flavour and is treated as a dual-purpose fig for fresh eating and drying. The eye (ostiole) is small and tightly closed, and the fruit resists cracking and splitting, which helps it cope with rain and humidity. It bears a light breba crop followed by a heavier main crop.

claims.text: Selected from Ira J. Condit's University of California fig breeding program at Riverside
Long slender neck, light tan to brownish-yellow skin with violet stripes, amber to pink pulp
Small, tightly closed eye and good resistance to cracking and splitting, with high sugar
Rich, sweet, dual-purpose flavour for fresh eating and drying, bearing a breba and a heavier main crop

sources.name: Four Winds Growers: Flanders Fig Tree

paragraphs: French Paillard (also recorded as Franche Paillard, with the synonyms Abondance and Franque Pagarde) is an old French fig documented by French pomologists from the late eighteenth century onward. It was among the European fig varieties preserved in California through the heirloom nurseryman Felix Gillet, whose original mother tree still grows near his former Barren Hill Nursery in Nevada City.

It is a two-crop fig with a light early (breba) crop and a very abundant main crop. The fruit is medium and pear-shaped, the skin violet-brown in the sun and greenish-violet in shade, and the pulp is red, very juicy and of very good quality. Growers rate its second-crop flavour highly and note it does well as a pot plant.

claims.text: Old French fig also recorded as Franche Paillard, with synonyms Abondance and Franque Pagarde; documented by French pomologists from the late eighteenth century
Preserved in California through heirloom nurseryman Felix Gillet; an original mother tree still grows near his former Barren Hill Nursery in Nevada City
A two-crop fig with a light early (breba) crop and a very abundant main crop
Medium, pear-shaped fruit; skin violet-brown in the sun to greenish-violet in shade; pulp red, very juicy and of very good quality
Rated highly for its second-crop flavour and suited to pot culture

sources.name: Figs 4 Fun: varieties beginning with P (Paillard)

paragraphs: Goutte d'Or, French for drop of gold, is an old French fig also recorded under the synonyms Doree and Figue d'Or. The name describes its golden-yellow skin, which can take on a rose to wine-coloured flush as it ripens, over pink flesh.

It is a common-type, self-fertile fig that crops without a pollinator and bears two crops in a season, an early breba and a later main crop. The flavour is sweet and fruity, and it is regarded as excellent for drying.

claims.text: Old French fig whose name means drop of gold; also known as Doree and Figue d'Or
Golden-yellow skin tinged with rose, becoming wine-coloured, over pink flesh
Common-type, self-fertile fig that bears two crops (breba and main)
Sweet, fruity flavour and regarded as excellent for drying

sources.name: Fig Database: Doree (synonyms include Goutte d'Or, Figue d'Or)
Promesse de Fleurs: Figuier Goutte d'Or

paragraphs: Hivernenca De La Senyora is a Spanish fig from the Balearic Islands (Mallorca), documented by fig researcher Montserrat Pons. The Catalan name 'Hivernenca' marks it as a winter fig: it is very late, beginning to ripen in October and carrying on well into winter, unusually late for a fig.

The elongated fruit is marked with distinctive cracks and shifts from green-yellow to a mix of brown, purple and grey as it matures. Inside, the flesh is deep red with a rich berry flavour that collectors compare to the prized Black Madeira, which has made it a sought-after variety.

claims.text: A Spanish fig from the Balearic Islands (Mallorca), documented by fig researcher Montserrat Pons
A very late variety; the Catalan name Hivernenca means winter fig, and it begins ripening in October and continues into winter
Elongated fruit marked with distinctive cracks, shifting from green-yellow to brown, purple and grey as it matures
Deep red flesh with a rich berry flavour that collectors compare to Black Madeira

sources.name: Fig Boss (Ross Raddi): De La Senyora (Hivernenca) Variety Review

paragraphs: Panache, also sold as Tiger or Striped fig, is a striking variegated cultivar. It is a sport (a natural mutation) of the Spanish fig Bordissot Blanca, recorded in fig literature as far back as Risso in 1826. The unripe fruit shows alternating green and yellow lengthwise stripes, the feature that earns it the tiger name. As the fig ripens the stripes soften and the skin yellows, while inside the flesh is strawberry pink.

On flavour, Panache is prized for a rich, sweet, berry taste often likened to raspberry or strawberry jam. It is a unifera type, meaning it makes a single main crop and no early breba crop. The fruit ripens late in the season, so it does best where summers are long. The striped skin and jammy red flesh make it a sought after fresh eating fig rather than a drying variety.

claims.text: Panache, also called Tiger or Striped fig, is a sport (natural mutation) of the Spanish variety Bordissot Blanca, recorded since Risso in 1826.
Unripe fruit has alternating green and yellow lengthwise stripes that fade as it ripens.
The flesh is strawberry pink.
The flavour is rich and sweet with a berry, raspberry or strawberry jam character.
Panache is a unifera type producing a single main crop with no breba, and ripens late in the season.

paragraphs: Preston Prolific is an Australian fig thought to be a seedling of Black Genoa that originated in Victoria, and it has been sold in Australia for decades. It is a vigorous tree with large leaves bearing large fruit on a short stalk. The skin is green when immature and changes from straw to a purplish brown when fully ripe.

Inside, the flesh is thick and juicy, described as creamy white to amber and sometimes tinted red. It is a late maturing variety, ripening through summer into autumn, and is used fresh as well as for jam and drying.

claims.text: Preston Prolific is thought to be a seedling of Black Genoa that originated in Victoria
It has been grown and sold in Australia for decades
The skin is green when immature and changes from straw to purplish brown when ripe
The flesh is thick and juicy, described as creamy white to amber and sometimes tinted red
It is late maturing, ripening through summer into autumn
It is used fresh and for jam and drying

sources.name: Heritage Fruit Trees: Preston Prolific Fig
The Food Forest: Figs fact sheet

paragraphs: Purple Vigilante is a heritage fig that has been grown in Australia since at least 1935. It was obtained by a Western Australian fig collector who passed cuttings on to private growers in Victoria, and it stays uncommon in the nursery trade. Like most common figs it is self-pollinating.

The fruit has purple skin and reddish-purple flesh, noticeably darker inside than Black Genoa. Growers describe the flavour as strong, sweet and complex, and rate it highly. It ripens mid-season and suits fresh eating, drying and jams.

claims.text: Heritage fig grown in Australia since at least 1935, obtained by a Western Australian collector who passed cuttings to growers in Victoria
Self-pollinating and uncommon in the nursery trade
Purple skin with reddish-purple flesh, darker inside than Black Genoa
Strong, sweet and complex flavour, ripening mid-season, used fresh, dried and for jams

sources.name: Fruit Tree Cottage: Fig Purple Vigilante
Daleys Fruit Forum: Heirloom fig varieties

paragraphs: Violette de Bordeaux is an old French fig, documented in France since the 1700s and also known as Negronne, Petite Aubique and Petite Negri, among other names. Genetic testing has shown that figs sold as Beer's Black and Vista are synonyms of it. The fruit is small to medium and pear shaped, with dark purple to black skin and deep red, jam like flesh.

It is one of the most highly rated figs for flavour, rich and sweet with a berry character that gives its name to the Bordeaux taste category. It produces an excellent early (breba) crop as well as a main crop, and despite a dwarf reputation the tree is actually vigorous and upright with deeply cut leaves.

claims.text: Violette de Bordeaux is an old French fig documented since the 1700s, also called Negronne, Petite Aubique and Petite Negri
Genetic testing showed Beer's Black and Vista are synonyms of Violette de Bordeaux
The fruit is small to medium, pear shaped, with dark purple to black skin and deep red flesh
The flavour is rich and sweet with a berry character, giving its name to the Bordeaux taste category
It produces an excellent early (breba) crop as well as a main crop
Despite a dwarf reputation the tree is vigorous and upright with deeply cut leaves

sources.name: Fig Boss: Violette de Bordeaux review

paragraphs: White Adriatic is an old Italian fig long known in its homeland as Verdone (greenfinch) for its green skin, and also called Strawberry Fig and Fico di Fragola. The fruit keeps a light greenish to yellow skin even when ripe, but splits open to reveal striking strawberry-red flesh. The flavour is balanced and sweet with light berry notes, prized when fully ripened on the tree.

Adriatic has long been the principal drying fig of California, valued for its high sugar and concentrated flavour when dehydrated, and it also makes excellent jam. In Australia it is grown as an early variety suited to cooler areas, carrying a light early (breba) crop ahead of the main crop, with fruit ripening around February in southern districts.

claims.text: White Adriatic is an old Italian fig also known as Verdone, Strawberry Fig and Fico di Fragola
The skin stays light greenish to yellow while the flesh is strawberry red
The flavour is balanced and sweet with light berry notes
Adriatic has been the principal drying fig of California and is good for jam
It carries a light early (breba) crop ahead of the main crop
In Australia it is an early variety suited to cooler areas, ripening around February

sources.name: The Food Forest: Figs fact sheet

paragraphs: White Genoa is an old, widely grown white fig described in literature as far back as the 18th century and imported into California in the 19th century. It carries greenish to yellow skin and amber flesh, often tinged strawberry, with the early (breba) crop showing lighter, more strawberry pulp than the main crop. In Australia it is valued as an adaptable variety that suits cooler and temperate areas.

It typically produces two crops where summers are warm enough, a smaller early crop followed by the main crop, in Australia often a light December crop with more fruit in February and March. The flavour is mild and sweet, and the fruit is good both fresh and for drying, though large soft fruit can be prone to spoilage.

claims.text: White Genoa is an old white fig described since the 18th century and imported to California in the 19th century
It has greenish to yellow skin and amber flesh tinged with strawberry
It produces both an early (breba) crop and a main crop where summers are warm enough
In Australia it gives a light December crop with more fruit in February and March
The flavour is mild and sweet and the fruit is good fresh and for drying

sources.name: The Food Forest: Figs fact sheet

paragraphs: Excel is a yellow fig that came out of the University of California fig breeding program at Riverside, the work associated with Ira Condit and colleagues, and was introduced in 1975. It is sometimes listed as a Kadota hybrid. The fruit is medium sized with yellow to greenish yellow skin and amber pulp, and it carries a small, tight eye. That small eye plus a tough skin make it notably resistant to cracking and splitting, even in adverse weather.

Flavour is sweet and rich, and Excel is regarded as one of the better flavoured yellow figs and a more dependable cropper than the Kadota it resembles. The split resistance and dense flesh make it a genuine all purpose fig, good fresh and also suited to drying and canning.

claims.text: Excel is a yellow fig from the University of California breeding program at Riverside, associated with Ira Condit, introduced in 1975 and sometimes listed as a Kadota hybrid.
The fruit is medium sized with yellow to greenish yellow skin and amber pulp, and a small, tight eye.
It is resistant to cracking and splitting even under adverse conditions.
The flavour is sweet and rich.
It is an all purpose fig, good fresh and suited to drying and canning.

sources.name: OC Fruit: Figs in Coastal Southern California

paragraphs: Zidi is a Tunisian fig and one of the country's most prized commercial varieties, traditionally grown in the mountainous Djebba (Mont el Gorraa) area on terraced hillsides. It is a Smyrna-type fig, which means it has an absolute need for caprification (pollination by the fig wasp) to set ripe fruit, unlike common self-fertile figs.

The fruit is large with tough purplish-black to violet-black skin and soft reddish flesh, prized for its high sugar content and rich, sweet berry flavour with a jammy, chewy texture. Beyond fresh eating, Zidi figs are sun-dried into 'chriha' for year-round use and folded into Tunisian pastries such as Magroud and Samsa.

claims.text: A Smyrna-type fig from Tunisia that requires caprification (fig-wasp pollination) to set ripe fruit
Large fruit with tough purplish-black to violet-black skin and soft reddish flesh
Known for high sugar content and a rich, sweet berry flavour with a jammy, chewy texture
Traditionally grown on terraced hillsides in the Djebba (Mont el Gorraa) area and dried into 'chriha', and used in pastries such as Magroud and Samsa

sources.name: Slow Food Foundation Ark of Taste: Zidi Fig
Fig Cultivation and Genetic Resources in Tunisia, an Overview
